summary: [session] Honestly? I think you're onto something real, and it's worth being precise about why, because it points at a bigger decision than just the search bar.
content:
Session: 168 user turns, 368 assistant turns
Tools: 155 calls (Bash×87, Read×38, Edit×26, Write×2, Agent×1, Skill×1)
Files touched: src/lib/map-data.ts, src/pages/map.astro, /tmp/map-desktop.png, /tmp/map-mobile.png, /tmp/map-zoom2x.png, /tmp/cluster3x.png, /tmp/m2-desktop.png, /tmp/m2-mobile.png, /tmp/m3-mobile.png, /tmp/m3-desktop.png, /tmp/fp-desktop.png, /tmp/fpcz.png, …

Final response excerpt:
Honestly? I think you're onto something real, and it's worth being precise about *why*, because it points at a bigger decision than just the search bar.

**Search is a *retrieval* tool. Your product has a *discovery* problem.** Those are opposites. Search only works when the user already knows what they're looking for and just needs to find it. But your entire pitch — the line on your own page is "most people graduate without knowing half of this exists" — is that people *don't* know what they have. You can't search for something you don't know exists. So a search bar sitting at the top of a discovery product is quietly solving the wrong job. Your instinct is correct.

**The one job it does actually serve:** brand-name verification. A student hears "wait, Dartmouth gives you free NYT?" fr…
